text:
# Page 1^page=1
TimePre: Bridging Accuracy, Efficiency, and Stability in Probabilistic Time-Series Forecasting Lingyu Jiang 1† Lingyu Xu 2† Peiran Li 3,4,5 Qianwen Ge 6 Dingyi Zhuang 7 Shuo Xing 3 Wenjing Chen 3 Xiangbo Gao 3 Ting-Hsuan Chen 8 Xueying Zhan 1 Xin Zhang 9 Ziming Zhang 2 Zhengzhong Tu 3 Michael Zielewski 1* Kazunori Yamada 1* Fangzhou Lin 1,2,3* 1Tohoku University 2WPI 3Texas A&M University 4University of Wisconsin-Madison 5UIUC 6Georgia Tech 7MIT 8University of Southern California 9San Diego State University Abstract Probabilistic Time-Series Forecasting (PTSF) is critical for uncertainty-aware decision making, but existing generative models, such as diffusion-based approaches, are compu- tationally prohibitive due to expensive iterative sampling. Non-sampling frameworks like Multiple Choice Learning (MCL) offer an efficient alternative, but suffer from severe training instability and hypothesis collapse, which has his- torically hindered their performance. This problem is dra- matically exacerbated when attempting to combine them with modern, efficient MLP-based backbones. To resolve this fundamental incompatibility, we propose TimePre, a novel framework that successfully unifies the efficiency of MLP-based models with the distributional flexibility of the MCL paradigm. The core of our solution is Stabilized In- stance Normalization (SIN), a novel normalization layer that explicitly remedies this incompatibility. SIN stabilizes the hybrid architecture by correcting channel-wise statis- tical shifts, definitively resolving the catastrophic hypoth- esis collapse. Extensive experiments on six benchmark datasets demonstrate that TimePre achieves new state-of- the-art accuracy on key probabilistic metrics. Critically, TimePre achieves inference speeds orders of magnitude faster than sampling-based models and, unlike prior MCL work, demonstrates stable performance scaling. It thus bridges the long-standing gap between accuracy, efficiency, and stability in probabilistic forecasting. 1. Introduction Probabilistic time-Series forecasting (PTSF) [28] aims to model the conditional distribution of future trajectories †Co-first authors with equal contribution *Corresponding Authors: Michael Zielewski, Kazunori Ya- mada and Fangzhou Lin (mike.zielewski@tohoku.ac.jp, yamada@tohoku.ac.jp, fangzhoulin1@tamu.edu) MeanScaler SIN(Ours) WikiElectricityExchange SolarTrafficTaxi Tactis2 TimeGrad TimeMCL(A) TimeMCL(R) TimePre(Ours) 0.02750.08750.1175 7774 15774 23774 267.06 347.06 427.06 0.68 0.88 1.08 182.27 262.27 342.27 263492 303492 343492 Figure 1. Top: Model performance comparison on the Distortion metric across six real-world benchmark datasets. Bottom: Visual- ization on the Electricity dataset comparing MeanScaler and SIN within the TimePre framework, illustrating SIN’s ability to place trajectories on the correct scale and support more accurate predic- tions. given historical observations, which is vital for applications in weather prediction [65], energy management [48], and finance. Recent advances in deep learning [32] have in- troduced several generative paradigms [21, 62] for PTSF, including diffusion-based models such as TimeGrad [50], flow-based models such as TempFlow [49], and copula- based approaches such as TACTiS-2 [3]. Despite their success, these approaches rely on costly multi-step sam- pling to represent uncertainty, limiting their efficiency andarXiv:2511.18539v1 [cs.LG] 23 Nov 2025

# Page 2^page=2
practical scalability. Recently, TimeMCL [10, 45] applied the Multiple Choice Learning (MCL) framework to PTSF, introducing a non-sampling formulation that models a fi- nite set of discrete hypotheses within an autoregressive RNN [7, 11, 40], thereby improving inference efficiency. In this framework, each prediction head is trained under a winner-takes-all (WTA) scheme, where only the hypothe- sis with the smallest loss for each sample receives gradient updates. While this competitive strategy encourages mode specialization among hypotheses, it also causes uneven gra- dient updates [51], where only a few heads receive sufficient learning signals while others remain under-trained. Con- sequently, TimeMCL tends to produce limited hypothesis diversity and unstable training dynamics, which ultimately degrades overall probabilistic forecasting performance. Concurrently, recent breakthroughs in Long-term time- series forecasting (LTSF) have demonstrated that minimal- istic architectures, such as simple linear or MLP-based net- works [52] (e.g., DLinear [70], RMLP [37], TiDE [13], and TimeMixer [61]), can outperform complex Transform- ers [43, 60, 75] in both accuracy and speed. These lightweight models have emerged as the new state-of-the-art (SOTA) paradigm for LTSF, offering remarkable efficiency and stability. Motivated by their success, a natural and critical ques- tion arises: Can the efficiency of modern linear backbones be unified with the distributional flexibility of MCL? Our investigation reveals a fundamental incompatibility. Apply- ing MCL directly to these lightweight architectures results in catastrophic training instability and immediate hypothe- sis collapse. We identify that this failure is dramatically exacerbated by the linear backbone itself. Unlike deep non-linear en- coders (e.g., LSTMs [20]) which provide implicit regular- ization and constrain features to a shared manifold [42, 71, 72, 74], linear projections lack any such coupling. Conse- quently, minor initialization discrepancies or inter-variable scale disparities, which are common in real-world data, are directly exposed and rapidly amplified during training. Un- der the competitive WTA loss, only a few ”lucky” hypothe- ses aligned with the correct scale receive gradient updates, while the rest stagnate, leading to irreversible hypothesis collapse. To address these challenges, we propose TimePre, an efficient probabilistic forecasting framework named after its stabilized pre-normalization design and direct multi- hypothesis predictor. At its core lies the Stabilized Instance Normalization (SIN), a mechanism precisely designed to pre-condition the input and resolve the incompatibility be- tween MCL and lightweight linear architectures. SIN per- forms adaptive, channel-wise rescaling to correct statisti- cal shifts [47] before they are amplified by the linear en- coder. This stability preserves meaningful hypothesis di- versity and prevents the collapse commonly amplified by linear encoders. As shown in Figure 1, extensive experiments on six benchmark datasets demonstrate that TimePre achieves new SOTA performance in predictive accuracy across proba- bilistic metrics such as Distortion, while the introduction of SIN strengthens results by correcting scale drift and pro- ducing more reliable forecasts. Adopting the direct predic- tion paradigm [23, 57] from LTSF allows TimePre to deliver one-step probabilistic forecasts with inference speeds sev- eral orders of magnitude faster than existing methods. Sta- bilized Instance Normalization also ensures reliable scaling as the number of hypotheses increases. Together, these ad- vantages position TimePre as a unified forecasting frame- work that bridges modern LTSF and probabilistic model- ing by jointly enhancing distributional flexibility, computa- tional efficiency, and training stability. Our main contributions are summarized as follows: • We identify and diagnose a fundamental incompatibil- ity between modern linear forecasting backbones and the Multiple Choice Learning (MCL) paradigm, revealing why this combination leads to catastrophic hypothesis collapse. • We propose TimePre, the first framework to successfully unify the SOTA efficiency of linear models with the dis- tributional flexibility of MCL. • We design Stabilized Instance Normalization (SIN), the core mechanism enabling this unification. SIN adaptively pre-conditions the input, ensuring stable optimization and balanced competition under the WTA loss, thereby deci- sively resolving the identified instability. • Extensive experiments on six benchmark datasets demon- strate that TimePre achieves new SOTA performance across probabilistic metrics. It is orders of magnitude faster than sampling-based models and, unlike prior MCL work, demonstrates stable performance scaling that deci- sively overcomes hypothesis collapse. 2. Related Work 2.1. Multiple Choice Learning (MCL) The MCL framework [17] provides a principled approach to modeling diverse outcomes under uncertainty. Initially introduced as an assignment-based multi-model scheme, it was later reformulated as a differentiable WTA loss [53], enabling each prediction head to specialize in distinct modes of the data distribution. From the viewpoint of op- timal vector quantization [12, 35, 45], MCL approximates the conditional distribution through a finite set of learnable codevectors. Beyond its early applications in computer vi- sion, MCL has been extended to domains such as reinforce- ment learning [6, 8, 55], speech separation [44], and time- series forecasting [10]. In particular, TimeMCL [10] adapts

# Page 3^page=3
this framework to generate discrete future scenarios, achiev- ing a balance between accuracy, diversity, and efficiency. 2.2. Time Series Forecasting Models With the rise of deep learning, autoregressive RNNs such as GRU [9] and LSTM [20] have largely replaced tradi- tional models [14, 19] for time-series forecasting by cap- turing temporal dependencies through sequential hidden states. However, their limited parallelism and susceptibility to vanishing gradients hinder long-horizon forecasting [38]. Transformer-based architectures [60, 64, 75, 76] later be- came the dominant paradigm by modeling long-range de- pendencies via self-attention [68]. Yet, recent studies show that their gains often arise from normalization and scaling rather than the attention mechanism itself. This has led to the emergence of lightweight MLP-based alternatives, which achieve comparable or superior performance through simple linear or feedforward architectures [26]. Due to space limitations, a more comprehensive review of related work is provided in the supplementary material. 3. Approach 3.1. Preliminary Notations and Problem Definition. We consider a multi- variate stochastic process {xt} T t=1, where xt ∈ R D denotes a D-dimensional observation at time t. Given a look-back window of length L and a forecast horizon of length H, we define the input–output pair: Xt = [xt−L, xt−L+1, . . . , xt−1]⊤ ∈ R L×D, (1) Yt = [xt, xt+1, . . . , xt+H−1] ⊤ ∈ R H×D. (2) The forecasting objective is to learn a mapping fΘ : RL×D → R H×D that minimizes the conditional risk: min Θ E(X,Y)∼D[ ℓ(fΘ(X), Y)] , (3) where ℓ(·, ·) is a suitable loss induced by the forecasting likelihood (e.g., mean-squared error for Gaussian noise), and D denotes the underlying data distribution. While the objective in (3) defines a deterministic map- ping, real-world temporal processes are inherently stochas- tic and often exhibit multiple plausible futures. A sin- gle predictor cannot adequately approximate the full con- ditional distribution p(Y | X). To represent such multi- modality in a tractable manner, we adopt the functional quantization formulation [17], where a finite set of K hy- pothesis functions {f (k) Θ }K k=1 jointly approximate the con- ditional manifold by minimizing the expected distortion: min {f (k) Θ } E(X,Y) [ min k=1,...,K d (f (k) Θ (X), Y)] , (4) where d(·, ·) measures trajectory-level discrepancy, typi- cally the ℓ2 distance. This formulation treats each f (k) Θ as a representative centroid of a distinct mode of p(Y | X), establishing a principled bridge between deterministic re- gression and probabilistic forecasting. This formulation follows the multi-hypothesis learning paradigm introduced in Multiple Choice Learning [17, 34] and extended for structured prediction [53]. Probabilis- tic forecasting frameworks such as DeepAR [54],MQRNN [63] and Deep Ensembles [31] share this principle of approximating p(Y | X) through multiple, diversity- regularized predictive hypotheses. 3.2. Diagnosis: The instability of Linear Backbones under MCL Our empirical investigation reveals a critical instability when integrating modern linear backbones [13, 69] with the multi-hypothesis framework (Eq. 4). This combina- tion does not typically diverge numerically (i.e., gradient explosion [5]); instead, it succumbs to learning stagnation and catastrophic hypothesis collapse [53], where most hy- pothesis heads f (k) Θ fail to learn meaningful representations. We diagnose this failure as a destructive interaction be- tween two coupled factors: (1) Inter-variable scale imbal- ance, endemic to real-world multivariate data, and (2) Un- constrained linear mapping, which lacks the implicit regu- larization inherent in deeper, non-linear encoders. Impact of Scale Imbalance on the WTA Objective. Real- world multi-variable data (e.g., Electricity, Wiki) contain variables with vastly different physical units and scales. Standard global normalization, z-score across the entire dataset for example, is insufficient. The winner-takes-all (WTA) [45, 53] competition in Eq. 4 is highly sensitive to the relative error scale. A hypothesis f (k) Θ randomly ini- tialized to perform marginally better on a high-magnitude variable (e.g., total energy load) will consistently ‘win’ the arg min operation, even if it performs poorly on all other variables. Consequently this single high-magnitude vari- able monopolizes the gradient updates, effectively starving the remaining K − 1 hypotheses of learning signals. This creates a destructive optimization dynamic where gradient flow is biased and unstable, leading directly to hypothesis collapse. The Role of Linear Backbones as Unconstrained Am- plifiers. Importantly, this failure mode becomes signifi- cantly more severe when the backbone is linear. Non-linear encoders (e.g., LSTMs [20], Transformers [60]) provide a form of implicit regularization, projecting inputs onto a shared, lower-dimensional manifold. This coupling miti- gates extreme gradient asymmetry. Linear backbones lack this property by design. They are unconstrained mappings; any scale disparities or initialization biases present in the

# Page 4^page=4
input are not attenuated but are instead linearly amplified and propagated directly into the WTA competition. Without a shared manifold to regularize their behavior, the K hy- pothesis heads compete in a poorly-conditioned, uncoupled feature space. Consequently, minor initial scale differences are magnified, causing most heads to become inactive and halting effective gradient flow, which results in irreversible collapse. Deriving the Requirements for a Stabilization Mecha- nism. This diagnosis dictates a specific set of requirements for any mechanism intended to stabilize the linear-MCL framework. The solution must: 1. Operate per-instance and per-variable (channel- wise): To correct the inter-variable scale imbalances that distort the WTA competition. Batch-level statistics (e.g. BatchNorm [25]) are unsuitable as they destroy instance- specific temporal dynamics. 2. Be robust to non-stationarity and outliers: Real-world time-series are rife with spikes and concept drift. A standard normalization (like InstanceNorm [58], GAS- Norm [59]) that uses simple moments (mean/variance) would be unstable, propagating this noise. 3. Be analytically reversible: To ensure the decoder can project forecasts back into the original data scale without information loss, preserving the physical meaning of the predictions. To meet these three criteria simultaneously, we propose Sta- bilized Instance Normalization, a novel layer that provides robust, channel-wise, and reversible pre-conditioning. 3.3. TimePre Overall Architecture. The TimePre is a three-stage mod- ular pipeline constructed to unify the computational effi- ciency of linear projections with the probabilistic output re- quired by the Multiple Choice Learning paradigm. Given an input context window X ∈ R L×D, TimePre generates K di- verse potential future trajectories { ̂Y(k)} through a sequen- tial process: Stabilized Instance Normalization (ϕ), Linear Temporal Encoder (Enc), and Multi-Hypothesis Decoder (Dec). Specifically, the overall pipeline is formulated as: { ̂Y(1), . . . , ̂Y(K)} = Dec(Enc(ϕ(X)) ), (5) where ϕ denotes the Stabilized Instance Normalization layer. This layer is critical for pre-conditioning the non- stationary input data, eliminating the severe inter-variable scale imbalance identified as the primary source of opti- mization failure for linear models under the winner-takes- all (WTA) objective.The resulting stable latent represen- tation is then efficiently processed by the linear encoder (Enc) before being mapped to the final set of diverse fore- casts by the multi-hypothesis decoder (Dec). This archi- tecture adheres to the probabilistic multi-hypothesis for- mulation of [17, 34, 46, 53], but critically replaces heavy recurrent or transformer backbones with computationally efficient linear projections and our novel, analytically re- versible normalization, achieving an optimized balance be- tween computational efficiency and complexity. Stabilized Instance Normalization (SIN). To satisfy the requirements derived in Sec 3.2, we introduce SIN. It achieves robust, channel-wise, and reversible normaliza- tion. Standard Instance Normalization [58] (i.e., Requirement 1) is vulnerable to outliers (spikes) or distribution shift, which are common in non-stationary time-series. A single extreme value can corrupt its mean (µ) and variance (σ2) estimates, destabilizing the normalization. To satisfy the robustness (i.e., Requirement 2), SIN com- putes statistics using a trimmed estimator. For each variable (channel) d, given the input x(d) = (s (d) 1 , . . . , s(d) L ) ⊤ ∈ R L, let s (d) (i) be the i-th order statistic (i.e., the i-th smallest value) of x (d). We define a trimming ratio p ∈ [0, 0.5) and set k = ⌊pL⌋. The robust statistics are computed over the cen- tral L − 2k values: µ (d) r = 1 L − 2k L−k∑ t=k+1 s (d) t (6) v(d) r = 1 L − 2k L−k∑ t=k+1 (s (d) t − µ(d) r ) 2 (7) σ(d) r = √v(d) r + ϵ (8) The normalization and its exact inverse (Requirement 3) are then applied to the original, unsorted vector x (d) using these robust statistics: ˜x(d) = x (d) − µ(d) r 1L σ(d) r , x (d) = ˜x (d)σ(d) r + µ(d) r 1L (9) This channel-wise operation (Requirement 1) pre- conditions the input, ensuring that the linear encoder re- ceives robustly scaled variables. This decisively breaks the scale-based gradient monopoly diagnosed in Sec. 3.2. Linear Temporal Encoder. Following the efficient linear- centric paradigm [70], we use a simple linear layer as the temporal encoder. This layer operates channel- independently, projecting the temporal dimension L, di- rectly to the forecast horizon H for each normalized chan- nel ˜x(d) ∈ R L: z(d) = W (d) ˜x(d) + b(d), W (d) ∈ R H×L, b(d) ∈ R H The final latent representation Z = [z(1)| · · · |z (D)] ∈

# Page 5^page=5
RH×D is the concatenation of these channel-wise projec- tions. Multi-Hypothesis Decoder. The decoder consists of K parallel prediction heads. Each k-th head contains two com- ponents: 1. A trajectory head f (k) θ (e.g., a lightweight MLP) that generates a distinct trajectory hypothesis ˆY(k) ∈ R H×D from the shared latent Z. 2. A confidence head g(k) θ (e.g., an MLP) that predicts the likelihood γ(k) of this hypothesis being the best one. ˆY(k) = f (k) θ (Z), γ(k) = σ(g(k) θ (vec(Z))) where vec(·) flattens the input and σ(·) is the sigmoid func- tion, ensuring γ(k) ∈ [0, 1]. 3.4. Training and Inference During training, for a given ground-truth sample (X, Y ), we first compute the trajectory-level reconstruction loss for each of the K hypothesis heads: L(k) = 1 HD ∥ ˆY(k) − Y∥2 F (10) The “winning” hypothesis k∗ is identified as the one with the minimal loss: k∗ = arg mink L (k). Relaxed WTA Objective. To directly mitigate the hy- pothesis starvation identified in Sec. 3.2, we adopt a ε- relaxed WTA objective [46, 53, 56]. This assigns the major- ity of the gradient (1−ϵ) to the winner k∗ while distributing a small portion ϵ uniformly among the K − 1 “loser” hy- pothesis, ensuring they continue to receive learning signals: LR-WTA = (1 − ε)L(k∗) + ε K−1 ∑ j̸=k∗ L(j), (11) Confidence Calibration. The confidence scores γ(k) (from g(k) θ ) are trained to predict the winner k∗. We use a standard binary cross-entropy (BCE) loss, treating k∗ as the positive class and all others as negative classes: Lscore = − 1 K [ log γ(k∗) + ∑ k̸=k∗ log(1 − γ(k))] . (12) Total Objective. The final training objective is a weighted sum of the reconstruction and calibration losses: min Θ E(X,Y)[LR-WTA + β Lscore] , (13) where β is a hyperparameter balancing accuracy and cal- ibration. Inference. At inference time, the model performs a sin- gle, efficient forward pass to generate all K hypotheses { ˆY(k)} and their confidences {γ(k)}. This ensemble forms the probabilistic forecast. Under the squared-error distortion, this entire framework can be viewed as learning a soft Voronoi partition of the future trajectory space, where each f (k) θ learns to represent the centroid of its assigned conditional mode of p(Y|X). 4. Experiment 4.1. Experimental Setup Dataset. We follow the standard evaluation protocol in mul- tivariate probabilistic forecasting and benchmark our model on six well-established real-world benchmark datasets from the GluonTS library [1]. These datasets cover multiple domains, including energy [67], finance [30], transporta- tion [36]. All datasets are preprocessed following the pro- cedures in prior works to ensure fair and consistent compar- ison across baselines. Solar and Electricity correspond to energy forecasting tasks with strong periodic patterns. Ex- change captures daily currency exchange rates, representing highly stochastic financial dynamics. Traffic and Taxi [73] cover transportation data, characterized by recurrent rush- hour peaks and short-term fluctuations. Wiki [41] contains daily page view counts of web articles, exhibiting irregular bursty behaviors and long-tail distributions. More details about the datasets are provided in the supplementary mate- rial. Metrics. We evaluate our approach using four metrics that assess both forecasting accuracy and computational ef- ficiency, namely Distortion [34], the Continuous Ranked Probability Score Sum (CRPS-Sum) [16], Floating Point Operations (FLOPs), and Runtime. Distortion serves as the primary metric, computed as the mean Euclidean distance between each target sequence and its closest predicted hy- pothesis, averaged over all test samples. Formally: D2 = 1 N N∑ i=1 min k=1,...,K d (F k θ (x i 1:t0−1), xi t0:T ), (14) where d(·) denotes the Euclidean distance, K is the number of predicted hypotheses, and N is the number of test sam- ples. This ensures a fair comparison across models when the number of hypotheses is fixed and directly reflects each model’s ability to generate accurate and diverse forecasts. In addition, the CRPS-Sum evaluates the overall quality of probabilistic forecasts by measuring the integrated dis- tance between the predicted cumulative distribution and the ground truth. Baselines. We compare with six representative probabilis- tic forecasting methods covering a wide range of paradigms, resulting in eight baseline variants. The selected base- lines include ETS [24], DeepAR [54], TimeGrad [50], TempFlow [49], Tactis2 [3], and TimeMCL [10]. Among them, TempFlow is implemented in two versions, based on LSTM and Transformer backbones. To evaluate

# Page 6^page=6
multi-hypothesis forecasting methods under consistent con- ditions, we include two WTA-based training variants, Relaxed-WTA and Annealed-MCL, for comprehensive comparison across all baselines. Training Details. Following the experimental setup of prior works, all models are trained using the Adam opti- mizer with an initial learning rate of 10 −3 for 200 epochs. Each epoch consists of 30 batches of size 200, sampled from historical data through random windows, where the context length is equal to the prediction length. Early stopping is ap- plied with a patience of 10 epochs, where training is termi- nated if the validation performance does not improve for 10 consecutive epochs. We rigorously reproduced all baseline models under identical configurations and training settings for fairness. All experiments are conducted on a NVIDIA RTX A6000 GPU with 48 GB of memory, averaging results over five random seeds (3141, 3142, 3143, 42, 43) for fair- ness and statistical significance. 4.2. Main Results Tables 1 and 2 summarize the quantitative results of Dis- tortion and CRPS-Sum, comparing our proposed TimePre with baseline models under 16 hypotheses. Figure 2 il- lustrates the computation–performance trade-off on the Ex- change dataset. Figure 3 provides qualitative comparisons between TimePre, TimeMCL (R.) and TimeMCL (A.). Distortion. Table 1 demonstrates that TimePre substan- tially outperforms previous SOTA models across multiple datasets. Specifically, it achieves a 38.8% reduction in dis- tortion compared to TimeMCL(R.) on the Electricity dataset and a 27.6% improvement on Exchange. Similar trends are observed on Solar and Taxi. Overall, we achieve SOTA per- formance across all datasets. On the Wiki dataset, although the improvement of TimePre over the baselines is less pro- nounced, we observe that several baselines fail to produce valid predictions. Detailed visual analyses are presented in a later section. CRPS-Sum. Table 2 reports the CRPS-Sum results across six benchmark datasets, comparing TimePre with existing baselines. Similar to the Distortion results, TimePre consis- tently achieves the best performance across most datasets, yielding the lowest CRPS-Sum values, and remains compet- itive on the Traffic dataset. Notably, the gains on the Elec- tricity, Exchange, and Taxi datasets are particularly substan- tial. Computational Cost. To evaluate the computation cost, we measure both runtime and FLOPs on the Exchange dataset. The results are shown in Figure 2. Owing to its MLP-based architecture and direct forecasting paradigm, TimePre demonstrates outstanding inference efficiency. Its inference time is the fastest among all compared mod- els, taking only 0.03s per batch. This speedup arises be- 0.12 0.10 0.08 0.06 0.04 0.1 1 10 100 200 TimeGrad DeepAR Trf.TempFlow TempFlow TimePre TimeMCL_R TimeMCL_A Tactis2 1.91×10 8,64.2s 2.90×10 4,0.25s 1.38×10 7,0.51s 5.81×10 6,0.29s 4.28×10 4,0.03s 2.51×10 7,11.8s 8.83×10 6,1.97s 8.83×10 6,2.06s Exchange Inference Time (s, log scale)Distortion Figure 2. Computation–performance trade-off on the Exchange dataset under 16 hypotheses. The x-axis shows inference time (log scale) and the y-axis shows Distortion (lower is better). Circle size indicates FLOPs, and annotations denote each model’s FLOPs and inference time. cause autoregressive models must iteratively generate fu- ture values one step at a time, whereas the direct forecasting paradigm produces all predictions in a single forward pass. In terms of computational load, TimePre requires 4.28×104 FLOPs, second only to DeepAR (2.90×104 FLOPs). How- ever, TimePre achieves substantially lower distortion, with a value of 0.0275 compared to 0.0796 for DeepAR. Visualization and Qualitative Analysis. We qualitatively compare TimePre with TimeMCL on the Electricity, Solar, Traffic, Taxi, and Wiki datasets. As shown in Figure 3, TimePre produces more stable predictions, whereas the an- nealed variant exhibits scale drift and the relaxed variant shows only partial improvement. On the Wiki dataset, TimeMCL collapses into nearly constant outputs and fails to produce meaningful fore- casts, while TimePre remains stable and captures realis- tic temporal variations. Although their Distortion scores are close, TimePre demonstrates superior qualitative perfor- mance, maintaining coherent dynamics and consistent scal- ing [27]. These results indicate that TimePre effectively stabilizes WTA training and enhances robustness in large- scale probabilistic forecasting. 4.3. Analysis of Different Normalization To assess the impact of normalization within our TimePre framework, we replace the Stabilized Instance Normaliza- tion module with three commonly used alternatives: Batch- Norm [25], LayerNorm [4], and GroupNorm [66]. The results in Table 3 and the trajectory visualizations in Fig- ure 4 reveal that normalization choice is a decisive factor for model stability and representational quality under the MCL paradigm. Training Instability. LayerNorm and GroupNorm exhibit pronounced instability during optimization. As shown in Figure 4, both methods produce latent trajectories with

Comparison across different normalization methods on the Electricity dataset in terms of CKA similarity, Distortion, and CRPS-Sum. LayerNorm and GroupNorm are shown with a wider y-axis range [−200, 400] for visibility, while our method and BatchNorm use [0, 200]. Our normalization yields stable trajectories, whereas the others suffer from noticeable scale distortion. liance on per-instance statistics makes them sensitive to the strong inter-variable non-stationarity in real-world time se- ries, leading to exploding or collapsing feature scales. Representational Inaccuracy. BatchNorm avoids diver-

# Page 8^page=8
gence but yields inferior forecasting accuracy. Since Batch- Norm aggregates statistics across a batch, it disrupts the per- sample temporal structure essential for precise forecasting. As a result, although trajectories remain numerically sta- ble, the model struggles to learn fine-grained temporal vari- ations, causing inflated distortion and CRPS-Sum. Latent Structure and Feature Geometry. To further un- derstand these behaviors, we analyze the latent feature ge- ometry using CKA similarity [29]. This metric quantifies the alignment between the learned representations. The re- sults in Table 3 show that SIN produces a noticeably dif- ferent CKA pattern compared with the standard normal- izations, indicating that it learns a distinct latent structure. Combined with the lowest Distortion and CRPS-Sum, this suggests that the representations induced by SIN are more suitable for the multi-hypothesis objective than those pro- duced by the other normalization layers. 4.4. Analysis of Multivariate Covariance To better understand the instability in multi-hypothesis fore- casting, we compare the covariance matrices of three set- tings on the Electricity dataset: (i) the original unscaled data, (ii) mean scaling, and (iii) TimePre with Stabilized In- stance Normalization. The unscaled data shows extremely large and saturated cross-channel covariance, revealing strong coupling and uneven feature magnitudes. Lower co- variance indicates more de-correlated and well-conditioned representations, which prior work [2, 22, 33, 69] links to improved stability. As shown in Figure 5, the mean-scaled variant retains dense cross-channel correlations, whereas TimePre yields a noticeably more de-correlated and struc- tured covariance pattern, reflecting a more stable latent ge- ometry. Origin Data Figure 5. A case visualization on the Electricity dataset compar- ing the covariance structures of the original data, TimeMCL with mean scaling, and TimePre with Stabilized Instance Normaliza- tion. 4.5. Ablation Study Effect of the number of hypotheses. To evaluate the stabil- ity and scalability of TimePre, we analyze its performance to different numbers of hypotheses K. As shown in Ta- ble 4, distortion results on the Electricity dataset are com- pared against the baseline models with both Relaxed and Table 4. Effect of the number of hypotheses on the Distortion Risk for the Electricity dataset. Results are averaged over five random seeds. Hyp denotes the number of hypotheses. Lower is better. Hyp TimeMCL (R.) TimeMCL (A.) TimePre 2 16012 ± 2310 15349 ± 2702 9201 ± 336 4 14311 ± 1234 13513 ± 1698 8864 ± 441 6 14173 ± 1432 11999 ± 624 8688 ± 783 8 13618 ± 793 12503 ± 1104 8590 ± 552 10 17216 ± 9112 12597 ± 2155 8066 ± 553 12 17277 ± 7886 12507 ± 820 8202 ± 259 14 13657 ± 2137 10902 ± 1152 7758 ± 480 16 12693 ± 1772 10335 ± 767 7774 ± 182 Table 5. Models Exchange Solar Taxi TimePre 0.0276 ± 0.0005 261.06 ± 1.55 182.27 ± 1.86 TimePre (D.) 0.0271 ± 0.0005 267.21 ± 2.79 179.82 ± 0.83 TimePre (M.) 0.0311 ± 0.0017 262.07 ± 5.55 169.22 ± 2.00 TimePre (T.) 0.0412 ± 0.0016 478.26 ± 73.69 174.18 ± 14.47 Annealed variants. As K increases, TimePre achieves lower distortion, indicating that it stably benefits from larger hy- pothesis counts through proper initialization and preserved head diversity. In contrast, TimeMCL exhibits unstable per- formance, and increasing the number of hypotheses does not consistently improve its results. As noted in its original study [10], this non-monotonicity arises from suboptimal mean scaling, which leads to poorly initialized hypothe- ses and uneven utilization. By contrast, TimePre demon- strates stable and consistent improvements as K increases, suggesting that its initialization and scaling strategy enable balanced utilization of hypotheses and robust performance scaling. Effect of Different Backbone. To assess the robust- ness and flexibility of TimePre, we conducted an abla- tion study by replacing its original single-layer linear back- bone with three representative MLP-based models: DLin- ear [70] (TimePre(D.)), TimeMixer [61] (TimePre(M.)), and TiDE [13] (TimePre(T.)). The experiments were per- formed on three benchmark datasets: Exchange, Solar, and Taxi. As shown in Table 5, different backbones perform best on different datasets. For example, TimePre(M.) achieves the lowest distortion on the Solar and Taxi datasets, while TimePre(D.) performs best on Exchange. Notably, the orig- inal TimePre, using a single linear layer, maintains com- petitive and stable results across all datasets, avoiding the severe degradation observed with TimePre(T.) on Solar. 5. Conclusion In this paper, we drew inspiration from recent MLP-based models in LTSF and extended their design to the multi- hypothesis learning paradigm. To resolve the inherent in- compatibility between linear architectures and the MCL

# Page 9^page=9
framework, we proposed the Stabilized Instance Normal- ization mechanism to harmonize feature scales and stabi- lize optimization. By integrating a linear backbone with a direct forecasting paradigm, TimePre achieves state-of-the- art performance across six benchmark datasets while main- taining extremely fast inference. Overall, TimePre unifies the strengths of deterministic and probabilistic forecasting, bridging accuracy, efficiency, and stability, and paving the way for future research in efficient uncertainty modeling.

# Page 13^page=13
TimePre: Bridging Accuracy, Efficiency, and Stability in Probabilistic Time-Series Forecasting Supplementary Material 6. Related Work 6.1. Multiple Choice Learning (MCL) The Multiple Choice Learning framework provides an ef- fective paradigm for modeling diverse outcomes under un- certainty. Originally proposed by Guzm´an-Rivera et al. [17] as an assignment-based multi-model training framework, MCL was later reformulated into a differentiable winner- takes-all (WTA) loss by Rupprecht et al. [53] to train multi- head neural networks, where each head specializes in cap- turing a distinct mode of the data distribution. From the perspective of optimal vector quantization [15, 39], MCL learns a quantized approximation of the conditional proba- bility distribution using a fixed set of codevectors [35], of- fering a principled framework for conditional distribution estimation. To mitigate the inherent instability of the WTA objec- tive to initialization and its tendency to converge to sub- optimal local minima. For instance, Annealed Multiple Choice Learning (aMCL) introduces a temperature-based relaxation to the WTA loss [34], utilizing simulated an- nealing to smooth the optimization landscape and improve hypothesis diversity. Similarly, Resilient MCL proposes learned scoring schemes to stabilize training and better han- dle ambiguous tasks where the number of valid modes is unknown [46]. In the context of probabilistic time-series forecasting, the TimeMCL framework adapts these MCL principles to gen- erate a discrete set of plausible future scenarios [46], ef- fectively balancing accuracy, diversity, and computational efficiency. Building upon this progress, subsequent studies have explored strategies to further enhance temporal consis- tency and multi-modal forecasting performance [44, 55]. 6.2. Time Series Forecasting Models Deep learning has precipitated a paradigm shift, autore- gressive recurrent neural networks [18] have largely re- placed traditional machine learning approaches [14, 19] in time-series forecasting. Models such as GRU and LSTM capture temporal dependencies through sequential hidden states. DeepAR, a prominent probabilistic baseline, lever- ages autoregressive RNNs to estimate parametric likeli- hoods (e.g. Gaussian or Negative Binomial) [54], allowing for robust uncertainty quantification. Despite their success, these models often suffer from vanishing gradients and lim- ited parallelism, making them inefficient for long-horizon forecasting. To address these issues, Transformer-based architectures were later adopted for time-series forecasting, leveraging self-attention mechanisms to model long-range dependen- cies. Models such as Informer, Autoformer, and FEDformer achieved remarkable performance on various benchmarks, establishing Transformers as the dominant paradigm in time series forecasting. However, subsequent studies revealed that the impres- sive gains of Transformers often stemmed from factors such as normalization [4, 25, 66] scaling, or data prepro- cessing rather than the attention mechanism itself. This observation led to the emergence of lightweight MLP- based alternatives, such as DLinear, TiDE, and TimeMixer, which achieve competitive performance through simple lin- ear or feedforward architectures without relying on atten- tion mechanisms. Recent research has also reignited the debate between channel-independence and channel-mixing strategies. While channel-independent models often gen- eralize better by treating multivariate series as univariate tasks, newer architectures like CSformer argue for a hybrid approach, combining channel independence with explicit channel-mixing modules to capture complex cross-variate dependencies without sacrificing robustness. 7. Experiment Details 7.1. Datasets We evaluate our method on six widely used probabilistic time-series forecasting benchmarks from the GluonTS li- brary, namely Solar, Electricity, Exchange, Traffic, Taxi, and Wikipedia. All datasets contain strictly positive real- valued sequences and come with standard train–test splits defined in prior work. An overview of their main character- istics is provided in Table 6. Table 6. Summary of the benchmark datasets used in our experi- ments. N denotes the number of time series, T the length of each series, and “Freq.” the sampling frequency. The prediction hori- zon H follows the standard settings in prior work. Dataset N T Freq. Horizon H Solar 137 7009 Hourly 24 Electricity 370 5833 Hourly 24 Exchange 8 6071 Daily 30 Traffic 963 4001 Hourly 24 Taxi 1214 1488 30-min 24 Wikipedia 2000 792 Daily 30

# Page 14^page=14
Solar. The Solar dataset contains hourly aggregated power production from 137 photovoltaic plants over roughly 7000 time steps. The series exhibit strong daily seasonality in- duced by the day–night cycle, making them a canonical benchmark for modeling periodic but weather-dependent generation patterns. Electricity. The Electricity datasetconsists of hourly elec- tricity consumption for 370 customers over 5833 time steps. Demand typically follows both daily and weekly cycles driven by human activity and business operations, and it can be affected by holidays and load spikes, which pose challenges for probabilistic forecasting models. Exchange. The Exchange dataset contains daily foreign exchange rates for 8 major currency pairs, each with 6071 observations. Unlike energy or traffic data, these financial series seldom show clear periodicity; instead, they reflect macroeconomic conditions and market events, providing a non-seasonal and highly stochastic forecasting scenario. Traffic. The Traffic dataset records road occupancy rates (bounded in [0, 1]) from 963 loop sensors, sampled hourly for approximately 4000 time steps. The series display pro- nounced rush-hour peaks as well as systematic differences between weekdays and weekends, which makes them a rep- resentative benchmark for high-dimensional, strongly sea- sonal traffic flows. Taxi. The Taxi dataset is based on taxi ride counts in New York City, aggregated at 1214 spatial locations every 30 minutes. We use the standard preprocessed version from, which includes data from January 2015 (training) and Jan- uary 2016 (testing). The resulting series capture complex spatial–temporal patterns and irregular spikes in demand. Wikipedia. The Wikipedia dataset contains daily page- view counts for 2000 popular Wikipedia pages. These se- ries exhibit a mixture of long-term trends, weekly season- ality, and occasional bursts due to external events or cam- paigns. Following prior work, we adopt the official split and treat this dataset as a challenging benchmark for high- dimensional, event-driven demand forecasting. Across all datasets, we follow the official train–test splits provided by GluonTS and prior benchmarks. For validation, we reserve the last few time steps before the forecast hori- zon within the training portion, as summarized in Table. 7.2. Evaluation Metrics We evaluate our approach using four metrics that capture both probabilistic forecasting quality and computational ef- ficiency: Distortion, CRPS-Sum, FLOPs, and Runtime. Among them, Distortion serves as our primary evaluation metric. Distortion.. Distortion measures how well the set of pre- dicted hypotheses covers the true target distribution. Given K predicted trajectories {ˆy(k)}K k=1 for each ground-truth sequence y, distortion is defined as the minimum Euclidean distance between the target and the closest hypothesis: Distortion(y) = min 1≤k≤K ∥ ∥ ∥y − ˆy(k)∥ ∥ ∥ 2 . (15) The final score is obtained by averaging over all test sam- ples: Distortion = 1 N N∑ i=1 min 1≤k≤K ∥ ∥ ∥yi − ˆy(k) i ∥ ∥ ∥ 2 . (16) Lower distortion indicates better probabilistic coverage and sharper forecasting quality. CRPS-Sum.. The Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS) [16] measures the accuracy of the full predictive distribution. Following prior work, we sum CRPS across all forecasted time steps to obtain CRPS-Sum: CRPS-Sum = H∑ t=1 CRPS (Ft, yt), (17) where Ft is the predicted cumulative distribution function (CDF) at time step t, and yt is the true value. CRPS for a single time step is defined as: CRPS(F, y) = ∫ +∞ −∞ (F (z) − ⊮{y ≤ z})2 dz. (18) Lower CRPS-Sum indicates better calibrated probabilistic forecasts. FLOPs. Floating Point Operations measure the computa- tional cost of a single forward pass. We compute FLOPs us- ing the standard profiling tools in PyTorch, covering all lin- ear, convolutional, normalization, and activation operations. Lower FLOPs indicate better computational efficiency and scalability. Runtime. Runtime records the wall-clock time required for one forward pass on a single GPU under identical batch size and sequence length. This metric reflects real-world infer- ence latency and complements FLOPs by capturing imple- mentation overhead and hardware-level optimizations. 7.3. Baselines We compare our method against a wide range of strong probabilistic forecasting baselines, including both classi- cal likelihood-based models and recent deep learning ap- proaches. All baseline implementations follow the official code from GluonTS or the authors’ releases, and we use the standard hyperparameters recommended in their origi- nal papers to ensure fair comparison. DeepAR. DeepAR [54] is an autoregressive probabilistic model based on LSTM networks. It predicts future val- ues by estimating a parametric likelihood (e.g., Gaussian

# Page 15^page=15
or Negative Binomial) and sampling from the learned dis- tribution. As a widely adopted baseline, DeepAR captures temporal dependencies through recurrent structures but may struggle with long-term dependencies due to sequential re- currence. TimeGrad. TimeGrad applies a conditional variational au- toencoder (CVAE) framework to time-series forecasting. By learning latent stochastic dynamics through diffusion- based variational inference, it provides expressive proba- bilistic forecasts. Its training, however, requires sampling latent variables per time step, leading to slower inference. TimeMCL. TimeMCL [46] extends the Multiple Choice Learning (MCL) framework to time-series forecasting by training K parallel prediction heads with a winner-takes- all assignment scheme. It generates diverse hypotheses that better cover the multimodal distribution of future trajecto- ries. Although effective, the dense latent representations in TimeMCL lead to high cross-channel covariance and less stable training on some datasets. TimeMixer. TimeMixer is a multi-period decomposition architecture that mixes temporal patterns across fine-to- coarse granularities. It performs especially well on sea- sonal datasets due to its frequency-aware decomposition and learned periodic mixing kernels. TiDE. TiDE uses a two-stage architecture: an encoder that extracts temporal representations and a decoder that pre- dicts the full future horizon in one shot. Its MLP-based structure allows for efficient training, although it can be sen- sitive to hyperparameter choices and normalization strate- gies. DLinear. DLinear is a highly efficient linear modeling baseline that decomposes the input into trend and seasonal components using two simple linear layers. Despite its sim- plicity, it achieves strong performance on many long-term forecasting benchmarks and is widely used as a lightweight baseline. For all baselines, we use the standard forecasting hori- zon defined in prior benchmarks and measure probabilistic performance using Distortion and CRPS-Sum (Section 7.2). Computational efficiency is compared via FLOPs and Run- time under identical batch size and hardware settings.
